---
title: "LLM Sentiment Analysis Comparison"
description: "Comparing BERT fine-tuning against Gemma 4 for classifying financial news sentiment."
author: "Woo-Sung Hollenhorst"
date: "2026-04-19"
image: ../../images/project1.jpg
categories: [NLP, Machine Learning, Deep Learning]
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
---
<style>
body {
  background-image: url('../../images/background.jpg');
  background-size: cover;
  background-position: center;
  background-attachment: fixed;
}
</style>

## Overview

This project compares two approaches to financial news sentiment classification: fine-tuning a pre-trained BERT model versus using a local Gemma 4 model with zero-shot and few-shot prompting.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Full Notebook
Download the complete Jupyter notebook with all code and outputs: [View Notebook](../../notebooks/sentiment-analysis.ipynb){target="_blank"}
:::

## Dataset Distribution

```{python}
import plotly.graph_objects as go

labels = ['Neutral', 'Positive', 'Negative']
counts = [3931, 3626, 2443]
colors = ['#5bcefa', '#7c73ff', '#e74c3c']

fig = go.Figure(data=[go.Bar(x=labels, y=counts, marker_color=colors)])
fig.update_layout(
    title='Sentiment Distribution (10,000 Sample)',
    yaxis_title='Count',
    template='plotly_dark',
    paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    font=dict(color='#e0e0e0')
)
fig.show()
```

## Methodology

- **BERT**: Fine-tuned `bert-base-uncased` with Optuna hyperparameter optimisation on a 3-class classification head
- **Gemma 4**: Tested with zero-shot and few-shot prompting strategies using the E2B variant
- **Evaluation**: Accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score (macro) across all models

## Model Performance Comparison

```{python}
import plotly.graph_objects as go

models = ['BERT (Optimised)', 'Gemma 4 (Zero-Shot)', 'Gemma 4 (Few-Shot)']
accuracy = [0.8000, 0.7480, 0.6834]
precision = [0.8001, 0.7664, 0.7380]
recall = [0.8100, 0.7645, 0.7118]
f1 = [0.8040, 0.7605, 0.6725]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Accuracy', x=models, y=accuracy, marker_color='#5bcefa'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Precision', x=models, y=precision, marker_color='#7c73ff'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='Recall', x=models, y=recall, marker_color='#2ecc71'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='F1 (Macro)', x=models, y=f1, marker_color='#f39c12'))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode='group',
    title='Model Performance Comparison',
    yaxis_title='Score',
    yaxis_range=[0, 1.05],
    template='plotly_dark',
    paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    font=dict(color='#e0e0e0'),
    legend=dict(x=0.7, y=0.1)
)
fig.show()
```

## Inference Speed

```{python}
import plotly.graph_objects as go

models = ['BERT (500 samples)', 'Gemma 4 (500 est.)']
times = [5.53, 50.5]

fig = go.Figure(data=[go.Bar(x=models, y=times, marker_color=['#5bcefa', '#e74c3c'])])
fig.update_layout(
    title='Inference Time Comparison',
    yaxis_title='Time (seconds)',
    template='plotly_dark',
    paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    font=dict(color='#e0e0e0')
)
fig.show()
```

## Error Analysis

```{python}
import plotly.graph_objects as go

categories = ['BERT correct, Gemma wrong', 'Gemma correct, BERT wrong']
counts = [84, 58]

fig = go.Figure(data=[go.Bar(x=categories, y=counts, marker_color=['#5bcefa', '#e74c3c'])])
fig.update_layout(
    title='Disagreement Analysis',
    yaxis_title='Number of Samples',
    template='plotly_dark',
    paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    font=dict(color='#e0e0e0')
)
fig.show()
```

## Key Findings

- **BERT outperformed Gemma 4** across all metrics when fine-tuned on domain-specific data, achieving 80% accuracy vs 74.8% for Gemma zero-shot
- **BERT was ~9x faster** for inference — 11.1ms per sample vs 101ms for Gemma 4
- **Gemma 4 zero-shot** performed reasonably well without any training, showing strong general language understanding
- **Few-shot prompting** did not improve over zero-shot for this task, dropping to 68.3% accuracy
- **Error patterns** showed BERT handled nuanced neutral sentiment better, while Gemma was stronger on clearly positive/negative text
